exemplar.title: How does the salinity of water affect the speed of light of various wavelengths travelling through it?

detailedExemplarData.title: How does the salinity of water affect the speed of light of various wavelengths travelling through it?

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: The research question is clearly stated, sharply focused, and consistently drives the narrative throughout the report.
The topic is communicated accurately and effectively with clear purpose and focus, and signposting keeps the investigation tightly on track.
weaknesses: A fundamental mis-application of Snellâs law (Equation 13) and minor methodological limitations (limited angle range, alignment issues) prevent full confidence in the methodâs completeness and appropriateness.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two strands (topic clarity and research question) meet the top band descriptors, but the methodological slip and limited measurement range lower the overall criterion performance into the 5â6 band. A score of 5/6 reflects the strong focus and question formulation, tempered by the marked procedural error.

strengths: Effective integration of Maxwellâs equations and oceanographic context demonstrates coherent application of subject knowledge.
Terminology such as refractive index, dielectric constant, and critical angle is generally used accurately and consistently.
weaknesses: Conceptual confusions (e.g. conflating optical density with mass density) and occasional undefined symbols (e.g. âkâ in Equation 11) introduce lapses in coherence and precision.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work shows good subject knowledge and mostly accurate use of terminology, aligning clearly with the middle 3â4 band. Conceptual inconsistencies and notation lapses prevent elevating this criterion to the top band, justifying a score of 4/6.

strengths: Data analysis includes error bars, RMSE calculations, and model fitting, demonstrating substantial analytical depth.
Arguments are logically structured, supported by data, and extended to broader climate and oceanographic implications.
weaknesses: The core mis-application of Snellâs law undermines the numerical validity of the analysis and its conclusions.
Peripheral digressions (e.g. optics market size) detract slightly from the sustained critical focus on the research question.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the studentâs analyses are sophisticatedâincorporating statistical treatment, anomaly discussion, and evaluative improvementsâthe fundamental calculation error compromises result validity. This places the work at the upper end of the 4â6 band rather than in the 7â9 band, meriting 6/12.

strengths: The structure follows IB conventions with clear sectioning, numbered figures/tables, and consistent formatting of headings.
Visual elements (graphs, tables) are generally well integrated, with informative captions and supporting error bars.
weaknesses: Pagination mismatches in the table of contents, low-resolution images, and occasional equation mis-alignment detract from overall polish.
Some unit inconsistencies (mg/L vs. mol/L) and missing precision guidance in tables cause minor reader confusion.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The reportâs structure and layout meet the descriptors for the 3â4 band, but recurrent small presentation lapses prevent it from achieving the highest band. A score of 3/4 accurately reflects this balance of clarity and minor formatting issues.

strandSelections.reasoning: Specialist termsârefractive index, dielectric constant, permittivity, critical angleâare generally used **appropriately and accurately**, and equations are integrated into the discussion.  However, there are **notable lapses**: optical density is occasionally equated with mass density, and variables (e.g., âkâ in Equation 11) are introduced without full clarity.  These inconsistencies prevent full alignment with the top band, situating the work in the competent 3-4 range.

The discussion moves logically from theoretical expectations to empirical findings, integrates graphs, and considers the broader implications for oceanography and climate.  **Arguments are generally coherent and supported by data**, yet occasional digressions (industry value, extended background) and the methodological error induce some inconsistencies in reasoning.  Consequently, the argumentation is strong but not fully sophisticated, corresponding to the lower end of the 7-9 band.

The student provides a **detailed procedure, control-variable table, safety discussion and justification of key choices (e.g., laser wavelengths, use of a semicircular tank, temperature control)**, illustrating an essentially complete method. However, a fundamental computational slip (Equation 13 mis-states Snellâs law, using arcsin(Î¸i/Î¸r) rather than sin Î¸i / sin Î¸r), limited angle range, and acknowledged difficulties with alignment and measurement introduce questions about the methodâs effectiveness. These shortcomings lower confidence in the appropriateness of the approach, placing the work in the 3-4 band.

The essay shows **solid understanding of relevant physics**: derivations from Maxwellâs equations, discussion of dielectric constant, Snellâs law and dispersion, and links to ocean optics.  Literature is used to frame expectations and compare results.  Nonetheless, several inaccuracies persist (e.g., conflating optical and mass density, misapplied Equation 13), and some symbols lack definition. These issues limit the demonstration of consistently excellent knowledge, so the strand aligns with the middle (3-4) band.

The student identifies limitations (measurement precision, mis-alignment, range of concentrations) and proposes **specific, feasible improvements** (laser mounts, larger tank, finer angle increments).  Percentage errors are calculated and linked to data reliability, demonstrating reflective thought.  Nevertheless, the critical mis-use of Equation 13 is acknowledged only implicitly, and the potential impact on conclusions is not fully quantified.  The evaluation is thus good but not consistently excellent, fitting a 7 in the 7-9 band.

The student presents a **clearly articulated and tightly-focused topic**ââHow does the salinity of water affect the speed of light of various wavelengths travelling through it?ââin both the title and introduction. The rationale is explicit, linking oceanographic relevance, climate implications and optical physics, and this focus is sustained throughout the essay. Section headings, signposting and regular reference to the research question make the purpose immediately apparent. Minor digressions into industrial market size are brief and do not materially weaken the overall clarity, so the strand meets the descriptors for the highest band.

The layout incorporates **integrated graphs, tables and images** that aid comprehension; captions are generally informative, and error bars are included.  However, several images are low-resolution, some captions are duplicated, and referencing within text occasionally misaligns with figure labels, causing momentary confusion.  Despite these issues, the visual presentation remains functional and largely supports the narrative, meriting placement in the 3-4 band with a score of 3.

The research question is **explicitly stated, sharply delimited (single variableâsalinityâtested over three fixed wavelengths) and consistently drives the narrative**.  Each section (method, analysis, conclusion) returns to this focus, demonstrating a sustained line of inquiry.  Although some ancillary climate commentary appears, it does not distract from the central investigative thread.  The formulation therefore fits the 5-6 band.

Data sets are plotted with **error bars, RMSE values and fitted to theoretical models**, and trends are compared across wavelengths.  The student discusses anomalies (e.g., 60 000 mg Lâ»Â¹ outlier) and evaluates uncertainty propagation.  The analysis remains tightly linked to the research question, though the incorrect Snellâs-law calculation undermines numerical accuracy.  Despite this flaw, the depth of statistical treatment and cross-comparison indicates good to very good analytical quality, meriting a score in the lower range of the 7-9 band.

The essay follows IB EE conventions: **title page, abstract omitted (appropriately), table of contents, numbered sections and labelled figures/tables**.  Minor issuesâfigure numbering mismatches, TOC pagination offsets and occasional equation mis-alignmentâdetract slightly from polish but overall structure remains clear and navigable.  Meets descriptors for the 3-4 band; assign 3 to reflect these small but recurrent lapses.

annotations.comment: Incorporating an online protractor method to reduce uncertainty exemplifies adaptive methodological refinement to enhance measurement precision.
The discussion acknowledges overlapping uncertainties that obscure wavelength-specific trends, reflecting mature critical evaluation of the data.
The term âoptical densityâ is conflated with mass density and refractive index, leading to conceptual confusion in the theoretical background.
The title succinctly communicates the investigationâs focus on salinity, wavelength, and light speed, establishing clear purpose and direction for the study.
The discussion of the $300\,$billion optics market, while interesting, is peripheral to the core research question and distracts from the central analysis.
The research question is explicitly stated and sharply focused on how salinity affects light speed for specific wavelengths, framing a well-defined investigation.
Deriving the speed of light from Maxwellâs equations ($v=1/\sqrt{\mu\epsilon}$) demonstrates strong integration of fundamental electromagnetic theory.
The table of contents contains pagination inconsistencies and duplicate entries, which may hinder efficient navigation of the report.
The introduction effectively links light propagation in oceanographic contexts to climate modeling, demonstrating coherent application of subject knowledge.
Figure 1 appears low-resolution and its labels are somewhat blurred, which may impede precise comprehension of wave properties.
Equation 13 misapplies Snellâs law by using $\arcsin(\theta_i/\theta_r)$ rather than $\arcsin(\sin\theta_i/\sin\theta_r)$, undermining the refractive index calculations.
The x-axis is labeled as mol/L while data are in mg/L, creating a unit inconsistency that could confuse interpretation of concentration effects.
The conclusion succinctly summarizes the rate-of-change constants (C-values) for each wavelength, directly linking quantitative results to the research question.
Table 3 presents processed refractive indices with associated uncertainties for each wavelength and salinity, supporting systematic comparative analysis.
Figure 10 integrates error bars, RMSE and correlation into the fit, demonstrating rigorous quantitative analysis linked to the research question.
The evaluation proposes concrete methodological improvements (laser mounts, finer angle increments), showing reflective consideration of experimental design.
The student reflects on initial concentration range limitations and justifies an expanded salinity range, demonstrating analytical planning and reflection.
The extension of findings to climate feedback mechanisms enriches the discussion with broader real-world implications.
The explanation linking refractive index to relative permittivity and electromagnetic theory shows coherent and relevant application of subject terminology.
The hand-drawn setup diagram lacks sufficient resolution and contrast, which may hinder accurate reproduction of the experimental configuration.
The table of calculated refractive indices omits units and decimal precision guidelines, introducing ambiguity regarding numerical interpretation.
Recognizing the circular tankâs geometry to ensure perpendicular laser entry and negligible glass refraction demonstrates effective control of systematic errors.
The salt-concentration independent variable is clearly defined with precise mass and volume measurements, establishing a robust experimental basis.
The correct form of Snellâs law (Equation 12) using refractive indexes and sines demonstrates accurate application of core physics principles.
